critic_raw:
Overall this is a well-constructed experiment. The temporal split, preprocessing discipline, and metric choice are all appropriate. I looked hard for fundamental flaws and found a small number of genuine concerns, none of which are fatal but one of which is real enough to flag.

**Issue 1: The label definition creates a structural label-leakage risk via the observation window**

The claim: The 14-day observation window beginning at day 14 yields a clean label. A learner is positive if they show zero events in any 7 consecutive days within days 14–28.

The problem: The dropout label is a function of *future* behavior within the 14–28 window, but so is the feature window (days 1–13 are features; days 14–28 generate the label). This is fine in isolation. However, the label operationalization says 'zero events for 7 consecutive days at any point during the 14-day observation window.' A learner who drops out on day 15 (first day of the window) will have a longer observable gap than one who drops out on day 26. More subtly: if the observation window ends at day 28 and a learner last logged in on day 22, they are negative — they will not have completed a 7-day gap by day 28. This creates a systematic survivor bias at the right edge of the window: learners with observation cutoff dates near the end of the 18-month dataset will have label suppression because the 7-day gap may extend beyond the data horizon. The temporal split does not fully address this; learners in months 16–18 of the test set may have right-censored labels if the data collection ended at month 18. This is not a leakage problem but a label quality problem that would deflate test performance specifically at the most recent cohort — the opposite direction of optimism, but it would bias the precision/recall curves in ways that depend on data collection cutoff rather than model quality.

Evidence: Compare positive label rate by calendar month of observation cutoff. If label rate drops systematically in months 17–18, right-censoring is present. The fix is to exclude learners whose observation window would extend beyond the data horizon, or to apply an explicit censoring flag.

**Issue 2: Bidirectional LSTM is epistemically misaligned with deployment**

The claim: A bidirectional LSTM on the 14-day pre-cutoff sequence is appropriate for predicting future dropout.

The mechanism of potential failure: Bidirectional LSTMs process sequences in both forward and backward directions. At training time, both directions process the complete 13-day event sequence (days 1–13 relative to the observation window). This is architecturally valid because at inference time the 13-day window is also complete — the model never sees future events beyond day 13. So the bidirectionality does not itself cause temporal leakage. However, it does impose an implicit assumption: that backward temporal dependencies within the pre-cutoff window carry predictive signal. This is a reasonable hypothesis (e.g., a learner who was active early but became dormant later has a distinguishable backward-reading pattern from one who was never active). The concern is not correctness but falsifiability: if the BiLSTM outperforms the LSTM only marginally, there is no ablation planned to determine whether backward processing contributed. The ablation suite described ('removing dwell-time features') does not include forward-only vs. bidirectional comparison. This matters because the bidirectional architecture roughly doubles the number of recurrent parameters, and any performance gain could be from capacity rather than bidirectionality.

Evidence: Add a unidirectional LSTM (same total parameter budget as BiLSTM) as a third comparison point. If BiLSTM ≈ LSTM (matched params) >> logistic regression, the bidirectionality is unnecessary. If BiLSTM >> LSTM (matched params) >> logistic regression, it is earning its complexity.

**Issue 3: Recall@fixed-precision is sensitive to threshold instability across courses**

The claim: Recall at precision ≥ 0.70 is the appropriate primary metric and is evaluated at a single global threshold.

The mechanism: The threshold is set globally to achieve precision ≥ 0.70 on the overall test set. But the per-course stratification analysis is secondary, not primary. If positive rates differ substantially across courses (a near-certainty given 'varying difficulty, pacing, and enrollment size'), a single threshold will produce precision well above 0.70 on easy courses and well below 0.70 on hard courses. The reported global recall@precision≥0.70 will be dominated by courses where the model is already well-calibrated. The design acknowledges this by reporting per-course recall at fixed precision as a secondary metric, but it is evaluated at the global threshold. A per-course threshold would be more operationally honest but is not described, and cross-course generalization (the stated goal) requires demonstrating that the precision constraint holds *within* each course deployment, not just globally.

Evidence: Report the distribution of per-course precision at the globally chosen threshold. If per-course precision variance is high (e.g., 0.45–0.90), the global metric is misleading as a deployment proxy. This would not change the experimental conclusion about sequence models vs. logistic regression but would weaken the operational claim.

**Summary of verdict reasoning:**

Issue 1 (right-censoring) is real and could bias test performance estimates in a direction that is hard to reason about without data inspection. It is a genuine flaw that should be addressed before trusting the quantitative conclusions from the test set. Issues 2 and 3 are legitimate gaps in the ablation/metric design but do not undermine the core comparison. I am rating this critique_wins on the basis of Issue 1 alone, because right-censoring affects label correctness on the held-out test set, which is the ground truth the entire evaluation rests on.
all_issues_raised: Right-censoring of dropout labels at the data collection boundary may suppress positive labels systematically in the most recent test cohort, biasing evaluation in a direction that depends on data horizon rather than model quality.
Bidirectional LSTM doubles recurrent parameter count relative to a forward-only LSTM but no ablation isolates whether the performance gain (if any) comes from bidirectionality or raw capacity.
Recall at fixed global precision ≥ 0.70 can mask substantial per-course precision violation when base rates differ across courses, weakening the operational deployment claim.
all_issues_adjudicated: Right-censoring of dropout labels at the data collection boundary may suppress positive labels systematically in the most recent test cohort, biasing evaluation in a direction that depends on data horizon rather than model quality.
Bidirectional LSTM doubles recurrent parameter count relative to a forward-only LSTM but no ablation isolates whether the performance gain (if any) comes from bidirectionality or raw capacity.
Recall at fixed global precision ≥ 0.70 can mask substantial per-course precision violation when base rates differ across courses, weakening the operational deployment claim.
